title: Social influence bias: a randomized experiment
authors: Lev Muchnik, Sinan Aral, Sean J. Taylor
claim_supported: Artificial initial positive social feedback can causally increase later positive ratings and final score.
key_result: Positive manipulation increased likelihood of positive ratings by 32% and final ratings by 25% on average.

title: Experimental study of inequality and unpredictability in an artificial cultural market
authors: Matthew J. Salganik, Peter Sheridan Dodds, Duncan J. Watts
claim_supported: Visible popularity information increases inequality and unpredictability of success.
key_result: Increasing social influence increased inequality and unpredictability; success was only partly determined by quality.

title: Field experiments of success-breeds-success dynamics
authors: Arnout van de Rijt, Soong Moon Kang, Michael Restivo, Akshay Patil
claim_supported: Arbitrarily bestowed early success can cause subsequent success across domains.
key_result: Early success improved subsequent rates of success compared with controls across funding, status, endorsement, and reputation systems.

title: Coordinated inauthentic behaviour on Facebook? A typology of manufactured attention
claim_supported: Timed repeated co-sharing can reveal manufactured attention networks beyond politics, including commercial and scam networks.
key_result: Coordination is operationalized as accounts performing the same action together at least r times within a time window t.

title: The coordination network toolkit: a framework for detecting and analysing coordinated behaviour on social media
authors: Timothy Graham, Sam Hames, Elizabeth Alpert
claim_supported: Coordination can be modelled as directed weighted multi-behaviour graphs.
key_result: The toolkit builds networks where accounts are connected when they repeatedly perform same or similar actions in a short time window.

title: How algorithmic confounding in recommendation systems increases homogeneity and decreases utility
authors: Allison J. B. Chaney, Brandon M. Stewart, Barbara E. Engelhardt
claim_supported: Recommendation systems trained on behaviour shaped by previous recommendations can create feedback loops.
key_result: Simulations demonstrate pernicious feedback loops and homogenization without utility gains.

title: A survey on popularity bias in recommender systems
claim_supported: Many recommender systems exhibit popularity bias.
key_result: Survey finds today’s recommendation algorithms often focus on already-popular items.

title: Dynamic feedback loops in recommender systems
claim_supported: Iterative feedback can magnify popularity bias, reduce diversity, and misalign systems over time.
key_result: Dynamic feedback loops progressively reinforce popularity bias and reduce diversity/personalization across groups.
evidence_type: Empirical evaluation with dynamic feedback-loop framework

title: The Impact of Fake Reviews on Demand and Welfare
authors: Jesper Akesson, Robert W. Hahn, Robert D. Metcalfe, Manuel Monti-Nussbaum
claim_supported: Positive fake reviews can cause consumers to choose lower-quality products and reduce welfare.
key_result: Experiment with 10,000 UK participants; welfare losses estimated around $0.12 per dollar spent in the studied setting.

title: The spread of low-credibility content by social bots
claim_supported: Bots can play a disproportionate role in early amplification and humans reshare bot-posted content.
key_result: Analysis of 14 million messages and 400,000 articles found bots amplified low-credibility content early before virality.

title: Threshold Models of Collective Behavior
claim_supported: Collective outcomes can change sharply when visible participation crosses individual thresholds.
key_result: Threshold distributions determine equilibrium participation; similar average preferences can produce different aggregate outcomes.

title: A simple model of global cascades on random networks
claim_supported: Small shocks can trigger rare large cascades in threshold networks under certain structural conditions.
key_result: The model identifies regimes where networks are susceptible to rare global cascades triggered by small shocks.

title: A genre trust model for defending shilling attacks in recommender systems
claim_supported: Profile-injection/shilling attacks manipulate recommender systems by pushing or nuking target items.
key_result: Describes shilling attack profiles and push/nuke attack logic against collaborative filtering systems.

title: Unsupervised contaminated user profile identification against shilling attack in recommender system
claim_supported: Recommender systems remain susceptible to carefully crafted profile injection attacks.
key_result: Proposes unsupervised profile-identification methods for attack profiles, including standard and obfuscated behaviour attacks.

title: Identifying link farm spam pages
claim_supported: Link farms can deteriorate link-based ranking algorithms and can be detected through graph structure.
key_result: Proposes algorithms for detecting link farms and improving ranking results through reweighted web graphs.
